W-Z8ijRwmQFG42z6EcxOmrpZJRE0wm6L7wvpy3VuK-XBck6pmpTDnFp8tdqdtzeQks5t7mfMdRr=
xmjshv4GUG-ObyRzexdz4-tnUxs9JgY6KFBUDXfgRgj4xGFkA2nOPrWr-G_6B9ooh5Y]. It st=
arted out a whole lot more boring than that: as a dongle that retailers cou=
ld plug into their phones to swipe credit cards.=C2=A0
Jim McKelvey had the idea in his glass making studio in St Louis, Missouri.=
 A customer came in with her eye on an orange-yellow, double-twist glass sp=
out for her bathroom. The spout had been gathering dust on McKelvey=E2=80=
=99s shelf for several years, so when the customer pointed to it, he was ke=
en to make a quick sale. Unfortunately, he wasn=E2=80=99t able to take her =
credit card, so the deal fell through. After she=E2=80=99d left, he looked =
down at his phone and wondered =E2=80=93 given what else it could do =E2=80=
=93 why that device couldn=E2=80=99t process credit cards.
McKelvey had known Jack Dorsey since Jack was sixteen. Jack=E2=80=99s mum, =
Marcia, ran a coffee shop that McKelvey would frequent. When he wasn=E2=80=
=99t glass blowing, he was managing a digital publishing business and, thro=
ugh Marcia, he roped Jack in as an intern. They kept in touch and in 2008 M=
cKelvey called Jack to kick around some ideas for a new venture. Jack had j=
ust been ousted from Twitter, so they agreed to pair up and pursue the dong=
le idea.=C2=A0
Jack and Jim=E2=80=99s Big Idea was less the technology inside the dongle =
=E2=80=93 which they didn=E2=80=99t even patent =E2=80=93 and more that the=
re was a huge untapped market out there of merchants too small for the trad=
itional payments industry to target. The key was to keep costs down, which =
they could do by aggregating the huge volume of small transactions that the=
se small merchants booked. At the time, there were 30 million businesses in=
 America doing less than $100,000 of annual sales; 24 million of them didn=
=E2=80=99t accept credit cards =E2=80=93 that was the pair=E2=80=99s market=
 opportunity.
They called their company Squirrel =E2=80=93 until they spotted someone els=
e had taken the name (that someone being Apple). So Jack did what he=E2=80=
=99d done at Twitter. Today, Twitch is the world=E2=80=99s leading live str=
eaming platform for gamers. But it was the original name of Twitter, in hom=
age to the motion people make when their phones buzz with a new message. Th=
e name didn=E2=80=99t resonate with the team so they opened up a dictionary=
 to find something close. From Twitch to Twitter. And from Squirrel to Squa=
re.=C2=A0
Over the next few years, Square would make itself invaluable to its custome=
rs. Drawing inspiration from Apple, it combined hardware and software into =
an integrated system. The dongle itself was given away for free and merchan=
ts would pay a very simple fee linked to transaction volume. The integrated=
 systems allowed Square to add new features that entrenched its position wi=
th customers =E2=80=93 features like working capital, invoicing, and payrol=
l, all to help merchants run and grow their businesses.
None of this was especially networky though. Just because one merchant has =
a dongle doesn=E2=80=99t mean the merchant next door needs to get a dongle.=
 Sure, the sleek white square may have looked cool enough for people to wan=
t one, but the social dynamics underpinning that weren=E2=80=99t intrinsic =
to the business model.=C2=A0
In addition, Square was giving up a lot of its economics. In payments, ther=
e=E2=80=99s a trade-off between being part of an open network and being a c=
losed network. The open network, operating on the rails of card association=
s like Visa and Mastercard, allows a cardholder flexibility to use their ca=
rd almost anywhere. But participants have to share the economics. In Square=
=E2=80=99s case, merchants pay a rate of ~3% on transactions, but Square ha=
s to distribute ~2% out to others in the network, leaving only ~1% for itse=
lf. In a closed network, the operator maintains their own ledger and can mo=
ve money from the cardholder side to the merchant side freely, retaining al=
l the economics for itself.=C2=A0
Square=E2=80=99s first attempt to bridge this gap was with Square Wallet. T=
he wallet gave consumers payment functionality which they could use at part=
icipating merchants. Square would be able to keep both the consumer side of=
 the transaction and the merchant side within its own walls. It wasn=E2=80=
=99t a success. Perhaps it was too early, perhaps merchant adoption wasn=E2=
=80=99t ubiquitous enough, perhaps Square didn=E2=80=99t invest sufficientl=
y in consumer acquisition. Whatever the reason, it was closed down. Its rep=
lacement, Square Order, didn=E2=80=99t fare too well, either.=C2=A0
That was fine. With a stable merchant business as its base, Square hasn=E2=
=80=99t been afraid to experiment on new businesses that have the potential=
 to both stand alone and ultimately bridge the gap. One such experiment tha=
t did stick was Cash App.=C2=A0
Putting the Network into Square=C2=A0
Introduced as Square Cash in 2013, Cash App was devised during a hackathon =
as a way to make peer-to-peer payments =E2=80=93 splitting the check at a r=
estaurant, sharing the cost of an Airbnb, that kind of thing. By the end of=
 2016, Cash App had 3 million monthly average users; today, it has more tha=
n 36 million.=C2=A0
Unlike the merchant side of the business, the consumer Cash App side exhibi=
ts very clear network effects. If your friend has it, and you want them to =
pay you back, you need it too. Square seeded these effects with viral marke=
ting campaigns and money giveaways, using Twitter as a particularly effecti=
ve acquisition tool. ARK Invest shows how [https://email.mg2.substack.com/c=
/eJxFkc-OhCAMxp9muGlAwD8HDnvZ1zAFOiMZRRfqbHz7RT1sQqD0S_uVHw4IX2s6zLZmYuc20r=
GhifibZyTCxPaMaQzeiLbppVYD80Z50euehTw-E-ICYTaUdmTbbufggMIarwrOG80m4wfPu6frh=
HoqUFwMwmmJ0nnbutJR376w-4DRocEPpmONyGYzEW35Ib8ezXdZkN5ViB_MVLt1uRIU3Iz5DCPM=
R6YqYUZIbiqp_LNDuVYO8lTBtlX0G84XFYkF0_BGcMmFkFpLXYvaisGjRit7q0QHzqLvVOe45T0=
MGuGh-PJq6rzbTODe5wgsmbiSXVOIryK_J1hwvoRCYiznssdAx4gR7Iz-hkQ36wvb-MKIqfyBH4=
EKYaFa3g5y4EN7QykUZdM3shMtK85-LVXx3_QPEPOXjg] Cash App=E2=80=99s monthly ac=
tive users correlates with its Twitter follower count. The strategy kept cu=
stomer acquisition costs exceptionally low: last year, they were less than =
$5 per customer.=C2=A0
In addition to viral marketing, the company reprised the playbook that fuel=
ed growth so effectively on the merchant side. That playbook has three elem=
ents: make the product free and simple to use, target an underserved custom=
er base, and layer in adjacent services.
In this case, the underserved customer base is the underbanked segment of t=
he population, for whom Cash App provides an alternative to a bank account.=
 Although the earliest Cash App adopters came for the network =E2=80=93 the=
 ability to pay their friends =E2=80=93 more recent adopters have come for =
the utility of a functioning bank account substitute. That=E2=80=99s especi=
ally been the case over the period of the pandemic where Cash App has been =
used to receive stimulus funds.=C2=A0
Layering in adjacent services is the key to unlocking economics in what is =
at core a free product. Over the past few years, Cash App has added lots of=
 new products: rewards (Boost), stock trading, Bitcoin, direct deposits, bu=
siness accounts, and Cash Card. The contribution these products make is bro=
adening. While only one of them made more than $100 million in gross profit=
 in 2019, four of them surpassed that in 2020.
But the model isn=E2=80=99t simply about cross-selling; it=E2=80=99s about =
engagement, too. Cash App benefits from the compounding effect of growing i=
ts customer base while also increasing engagement and monetization per cust=
omer. Around a quarter of Cash App monthly active customers engage on a dai=
ly basis. That=E2=80=99s not as high as Twitter, but for financial services=
, it=E2=80=99s pretty good.=C2=A0
In the fourth quarter, the average gross profit per customer reached $41. C=
ustomers who use multiple products generate 3-4 times higher gross profit t=
han the average. Cash Card is the most popular =E2=80=93 one in four Cash A=
pp customers use it =E2=80=93 but Bitcoin is catching up, at one in ten. Th=
e compounding comes from the higher rates of engagement customers with mult=
iple products produce. Those who use the Boost rewards feature, for example=
, spend two times more on their Cash Card than other customers.=C2=A0
Acquire, engage, sell. It=E2=80=99s a simple but highly effective model.=C2=
=A0
This year, Square is going to turbocharge that model:
They plan to do $800 to $900 million of incremental investment.
They continue to look for new ways to connect product lines within Cash App.
And they are getting closer to being able to mesh the consumer facing Cash =
App side of the business with the merchant facing seller side.
Cash App began 2020 contributing just over a quarter of the group=E2=80=99s=
 combined gross profit, but it left 2020 contributing nearly a half. As it =
hits scale there will be opportunities to close up pieces of the network, p=
icking up where Square Wallet failed. Jack said on his recent earnings call=
, =E2=80=9CWe=E2=80=99ve done a lot of internal connections between the two=
 ecosystems, and now we=E2=80=99re focused on more of the customer-facing c=
onnections.=E2=80=9D No doubt we=E2=80=99ll hear about it [https://email.mg=
2.substack.com/c/eJxFkNuOhCAMhp9muDQcFS-42Gyyr2E4VCWjYKDuxLdfZrzYhECb8vdvP2=
8Rllwuc-SK5H1NeB1gErzqBohQyFmhTDEY1nMtlBxJMDIwrTSJdZoLwG7jZrCcQI7TbdFbjDl9F=
JRyRVZjNdM2MMflOPBZ-X6mgg-Si1n1YZ757WvPECF5MPAL5coJyGZWxKM-xNeD_7SDr_ieqPN5=

XGksme9qMY6djf6zdegmsuBtaT5hxyU6V_0z9_E4uw] on Twitter when it happens.

7;s self-removal from the weeds really does allow his companies to make bet=
ter strategic decisions. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16=
px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">As I wrote in <em><a href=3D"htt=
ps://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkNuOhCAMQL9meNNwU_SBh33Z3zBcOkpWwUAd498vM=
ya7SWkTmvbAcQZhTvnSeypI3mnCawcd4SwrIEImR4E8Ba9ZzwfRyZF4LT0buoGEMj0zwGbCqjEf=
QPbDrsEZDCl-JijlHVm0s9QISY2n3jAlDRW8V3xQTinaP5W8uebwAaIDDS_IV4pAVr0g7uUhvh7=
8u8Z5nm1MaFMOcW5dqld7PUs6GzzD-7HNnLAJWJo5p_SCxhr3Q4LmlDMqKGOi60TXstay0UMHVg=
xWMmWcBa-kctTSwYwdmIek28zbctiCdUVlbSTrP3Zt_yxmg_XTqL-eat2OGPCaIBq7gr-F4O31o=
2iaIUKuvv1ksNpksqf9KEY69reAakzwgQvFelLJPtWp-A_9BZEnj_4" style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">How Twitter Got Its Groove Back</a>,</em=
> “Whether accidentally or through an ultra-attuned third eye and a Z=
en-like ability to withstand half a decade’s worth of criticism, he&#=
8217;s put Twitter in a surprisingly strong strategic position.”&nbsp=
;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin=
: 0 0 1em 0;">In other words, maybe Jack is running Twitter a lot more like=
 he’s run Square than meets the eye. </p><h3 style=3D"-moz-osx-f=
ont-smoothing: antialiased; -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased; color: #1a=
1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont,=
 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, 'Apple Color Emoji', 'Se=
goe UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol'; font-size: 1.375em; font-weight: bold; li=
ne-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong>Back to Square One&nbs=
p;</strong></h3><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 2=
6px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Square never started out imagining that one day it=
s product would feature in <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxF=
kE1uxCAMhU8TlhFgSMKCRaVqrhHx40lQE0iBtMrty0wWlSxb1pP1_D5nKi4pX_pIpZJXm-t1oI7=
4WzasFTM5C-Y5eM0GPoEUingtPJvkREKZnxlxN2HTNZ9IjtNuwZkaUnxfUMolWbWSADAxwYEO0n=
gF4zAyJ0agCBSe8vY1pw8YHWr8wXyliGTTa61H6eCj449WC8Zwlt6lvS0FTXZrB4_vDj6dKWvHJ=
afmOEjQnHJGgTIGUoLsWW-Z8ijRwmQFG42z6EcxOmrpZJRE0wm6L7wvpy3VuK-XBck6pmpTDnFp=
8tdqdtzeQks5t7mfMdRrxmjshv4GUG-ObyRzexdz4-tnUxs9JgY6KFBUDXfgRgj4xGFkA2nOPrW=
r-G_6B9ooh5Y" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">hip-hop=
 lyrics</a>. It started out a whole lot more boring than that: as a dongle =
that retailers could plug into their phones to swipe credit cards. </p=
><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 =
0 1em 0;">Jim McKelvey had the idea in his glass making studio in St Louis,=
 Missouri. A customer came in with her eye on an orange-yellow, double-twis=
t glass spout for her bathroom. The spout had been gathering dust on McKelv=
ey’s shelf for several years, so when the customer pointed to it, he =
was keen to make a quick sale. Unfortunately, he wasn’t able to take =
her credit card, so the deal fell through. After she’d left, he looke=
d down at his phone and wondered – given what else it could do &#8211=
; why that device couldn’t process credit cards.</p><p style=3D"color=
: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">McKelvey=
 had known Jack Dorsey since Jack was sixteen. Jack’s mum, Marcia, ra=
n a coffee shop that McKelvey would frequent. When he wasn’t glass bl=
owing, he was managing a digital publishing business and, through Marcia, h=
e roped Jack in as an intern. They kept in touch and in 2008 McKelvey calle=
d Jack to kick around some ideas for a new venture. Jack had just been oust=
ed from Twitter, so they agreed to pair up and pursue the dongle idea.&nbsp=
;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin=
: 0 0 1em 0;">Jack and Jim’s Big Idea was less the technology inside =
the dongle – which they didn’t even patent – and more tha=
t there was a huge untapped market out there of merchants too small for the=
 traditional payments industry to target. The key was to keep costs down, w=
hich they could do by aggregating the huge volume of small transactions tha=
t these small merchants booked. At the time, there were 30 million business=
es in America doing less than $100,000 of annual sales; 24 million of them =
didn’t accept credit cards – that was the pair’s market o=
pportunity.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26=
px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">They called their company Squirrel – until th=
ey spotted someone else had taken the name (that someone being Apple). So J=
ack did what he’d done at Twitter. Today, Twitch is the world’s=
 leading live streaming platform for gamers. But it was the original name o=
f Twitter, in homage to the motion people make when their phones buzz with =
a new message. The name didn’t resonate with the team so they opened =
up a dictionary to find something close. From Twitch to Twitter. And from S=
quirrel to Square. </p><div class=3D"captioned-image-container-static"=
 style=3D"font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin-bottom: 16px; text-ali=

ht: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Over the next few years, Square would make it=
self invaluable to its customers. Drawing inspiration from Apple, it combin=
ed hardware and software into an integrated system. The dongle itself was g=
iven away for free and merchants would pay a very simple fee linked to tran=
saction volume. The integrated systems allowed Square to add new features t=
hat entrenched its position with customers – features like working ca=
pital, invoicing, and payroll, all to help merchants run and grow their bus=
inesses.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px;=
 margin: 0 0 1em 0;">None of this was especially <em>networky</em> though. =
Just because one merchant has a dongle doesn’t mean the merchant next=
 door needs to get a dongle. Sure, the sleek white square may have looked c=
ool enough for people to want one, but the social dynamics underpinning tha=
t weren’t intrinsic to the business model. </p><p style=3D"color=
: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In addit=
ion, Square was giving up a lot of its economics. <strong>In payments, ther=
e’s a trade-off between being part of an open network and being a clo=
sed network. </strong>The open network, operating on the rails of card asso=
ciations like Visa and Mastercard, allows a cardholder flexibility to use t=
heir card almost anywhere. But participants have to share the economics. In=
 Square’s case, merchants pay a rate of ~3% on transactions, but Squa=
re has to distribute ~2% out to others in the network, leaving only ~1% for=
 itself. In a closed network, the operator maintains their own ledger and c=
an move money from the cardholder side to the merchant side freely, retaini=
ng all the economics for itself. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-=
size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Square’s first att=
empt to bridge this gap was with Square Wallet. The wallet gave consumers p=
ayment functionality which they could use at participating merchants. Squar=
e would be able to keep both the consumer side of the transaction and the m=
erchant side within its own walls. It wasn’t a success. Perhaps it wa=
s too early, perhaps merchant adoption wasn’t ubiquitous enough, perh=
aps Square didn’t invest sufficiently in consumer acquisition. Whatev=
er the reason, it was closed down. Its replacement, Square Order, didn&#821=
7;t fare too well, either. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: =
16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">That was fine. <strong>With a =
stable merchant business as its base, Square hasn’t been afraid to ex=
periment on new businesses that have the potential to both stand alone and =
ultimately bridge the gap.</strong> One such experiment that did stick was =
Cash App. </p><h3 style=3D"-moz-osx-font-smoothing: antialiased; -webk=
it-font-smoothing: antialiased; color: #1a1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Di=
splay', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, A=
rial, sans-serif, 'Apple Color Emoji', 'Segoe UI Emoji', 'Segoe UI Symbol';=
 font-size: 1.375em; font-weight: bold; line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 =
0.625em 0;"><strong>Putting the Network into Square </strong></h3><p s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em=
 0;">Introduced as Square Cash in 2013, Cash App was devised during a hacka=
thon as a way to make peer-to-peer payments – splitting the check at =
a restaurant, sharing the cost of an Airbnb, that kind of thing. By the end=
 of 2016, Cash App had 3 million monthly average users; <strong>today, it h=
as more than 36 million</strong>. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Unlike the merchant sid=
e of the business, the consumer Cash App side exhibits very clear network e=
ffects. If your friend has it, and you want them to pay you back, you need =
it too. Square seeded these effects with viral marketing campaigns and mone=
y giveaways, <strong>using Twitter as a particularly effective acquisition =
tool</strong>. ARK Invest <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFk=

3g5y4EN7QykUZdM3shMtK85-LVXx3_QPEPOXjg" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decor=
ation: underline;">shows how</a> Cash App’s monthly active users corr=
elates with its Twitter follower count. The strategy kept customer acquisit=
ion costs exceptionally low: last year, they were less than $5 per customer=

div><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin:=
 0 0 1em 0;">In addition to viral marketing, the company reprised the playb=
ook that fueled growth so effectively on the merchant side. <strong>That pl=
aybook has three elements: make the product free and simple to use, target =
an underserved customer base, and layer in adjacent services.</strong></p><=
p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 =
1em 0;">In this case, the underserved customer base is the underbanked segm=
ent of the population, for whom Cash App provides an alternative to a bank =
account. Although the earliest Cash App adopters came for the network &#821=
1; the ability to pay their friends – more recent adopters have come =
for the utility of a functioning bank account substitute. That’s espe=
cially been the case over the period of the pandemic where Cash App has bee=
n used to receive stimulus funds. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Layering in adjacent se=
rvices is the key to unlocking economics in what is at core a free product.=
 <strong>Over the past few years, Cash App has added lots of new products: =
rewards (Boost), stock trading, Bitcoin, direct deposits, business accounts=
, and Cash Card.</strong> The contribution these products make is broadenin=
g. While only one of them made more than $100 million in gross profit in 20=
19, four of them surpassed that in 2020.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; fon=
t-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But the model isn&#821=
7;t simply about cross-selling; it’s about engagement, too. Cash App =
benefits from the compounding effect of growing its customer base while als=
o increasing engagement and monetization per customer. Around a quarter of =
Cash App monthly active customers engage on a daily basis. That’s not=
 as high as Twitter, but for financial services, it’s pretty good.&nb=
sp;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; marg=
in: 0 0 1em 0;">In the fourth quarter, the average gross profit per custome=
r reached $41. Customers who use multiple products generate 3-4 times highe=
r gross profit than the average. Cash Card is the most popular – one =
in four Cash App customers use it – but Bitcoin is catching up, at on=
e in ten. The compounding comes from the higher rates of engagement custome=
rs with multiple products produce. Those who use the Boost rewards feature,=
 for example, spend two times more on their Cash Card than other customers.=
 </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; m=
argin: 0 0 1em 0;"><strong>Acquire, engage, sell. It’s a simple but h=
ighly effective model. </strong></p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-s=
ize: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">This year, Square is goin=
g to turbocharge that model:</p><ul style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"m=
argin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"colo=
r: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">They plan to do=
 $800 to $900 million of incremental investment.</p></li><li style=3D"margi=
n: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #=
1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">They continue to lo=
ok for new ways to connect product lines within Cash App.</p></li><li style=
=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D=
"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">And they a=
re getting closer to being able to mesh the consumer facing Cash App side o=
f the business with the merchant facing seller side.</p></li></ul><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"=
><strong>Cash App began 2020 contributing just over a quarter of the group&=
#8217;s combined gross profit, but it left 2020 contributing nearly a half.=
</strong> As it hits scale there will be <strong>opportunities to close up =
pieces of the network</strong>, picking up where Square Wallet failed. Jack=
 said on his recent earnings call, “We’ve done a lot of interna=
l connections between the two ecosystems, and now we’re focused on mo=
re of the customer-facing connections.” No doubt <a href=3D"https://e=
mail.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkNuOhCAMhp9muDQcFS-42Gyyr2E4VCWjYKDuxLdfZrzYhEC=
b8vdvP28Rllwuc-SK5H1NeB1gErzqBohQyFmhTDEY1nMtlBxJMDIwrTSJdZoLwG7jZrCcQI7Tbd=
